beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Lock onto a nearby star against the background sky. Come back in six months — Earth has moved to the other side of its orbit. The star appears to have shifted sideways by a tiny angle. That shift is the entire ruler used to measure its distance.

card.copy: A Star's Wobble Is Its Distance

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Lock onto a nearby star against the background sky. Come back in six months — Earth has moved to the other side of its orbit. The star appears to have shifted s

beats.narration_text: This is stellar parallax. Hold your thumb up and blink alternate eyes — your thumb appears to shift against the background. The closer your thumb, the larger the apparent shift. Stars work the same way.

production_viz.label: Thumb-blink analogy morphing into Earth's two orbital positions — parallax angle illustrated

beats.narration_text: What is the minimum information needed to convert a parallax angle into a distance — and how was the parsec defined to make the arithmetic simple?

card.copy: Star appears to shift when Earth moves 1 AU. How do you convert that tiny angle into a distance in light-years or parsecs?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: What is the minimum information needed to convert a parallax angle into a distance — and how was the parsec defined to make the arithmetic simple?

beats.narration_text: The geometry: Earth's orbit has a radius of 1 astronomical unit. Over 6 months, Earth shifts by 2 AU. The star appears to move against background stars. The half-angle of that apparent motion is the parallax angle p in arcseconds.

production_viz.label: Parallax geometry — right triangle with 1 AU base and parallax angle p at star vertex

beats.narration_text: Distance in parsecs equals 1 divided by p in arcseconds. This is the defining formula. A parsec is the distance at which a parallax angle of exactly 1 arcsecond corresponds to a 1 AU baseline — about 3.26 light-years.

production_viz.label: d = 1/p formula — parsec definition: 1 arcsec parallax from 1 AU baseline = 3.26 ly

beats.narration_text: Proxima Centauri: the half-swing parallax is 0.7687 arcseconds. Distance equals 1 divided by 0.7687 equals 1.30 parsecs equals 4.24 light-years. The nearest star's distance comes from a half-arcsecond wobble.

production_viz.label: Proxima Centauri worked example: p = 0.7687 arcsec → d = 1/0.7687 = 1.30 pc = 4.24 ly

beats.narration_text: Sirius: parallax 0.379 arcseconds — 2.64 parsecs, 8.6 light-years. Alpha Centauri: 0.742 arcseconds, 1.35 parsecs. Each is a direct geometric distance measurement requiring no assumptions about the star's physics.

production_viz.label: Comparison table: Proxima / Alpha Centauri / Sirius parallax angles and computed distances

beats.narration_text: The limit: parallax becomes unmeasurable when the angle drops below the telescope's resolution. Ground-based: useful to about 100 parsecs. Hipparcos satellite: about 500 parsecs. Gaia: 10,000 parsecs with microarcsecond precision.

production_viz.label: Distance ladder first rung — ground ~100 pc, Hipparcos ~500 pc, Gaia ~10,000 pc precision comparison

beats.narration_text: Beyond parallax range, astronomers use the distance ladder — each rung calibrated by the previous. Parallax is the first rung: the only direct geometric measurement. Everything further depends on getting this right.

production_viz.label: Cosmic distance ladder showing parallax as first rung — subsequent rungs calibrated from it

beats.narration_text: d equals 1 over p, in parsecs and arcseconds. Proxima Centauri: p equals 0.7687 arcseconds, d equals 1.30 parsecs equals 4.24 light-years. Parsec defined: 1 arcsecond parallax from 1 AU baseline. Ground limit: about 100 parsecs. Gaia: about 10,000 parsecs.

card.copy: d = 1/p. Proxima Centauri: p = 0.7687 arcsec → 1.30 pc = 4.24 ly. Parsec: 1 AU baseline subtends 1 arcsec. First rung of cosmic distance ladder.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: d equals 1 over p, in parsecs and arcseconds. Proxima Centauri: p equals 0.7687 arcseconds, d equals 1.30 parsecs equals 4.24 light-years. Parsec defined: 1 arc

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
